subject: Tort Law
lesson_title: Product Liability at Common Law, the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, and the EU Product Liability Directive 2024

questions.question: What principle of product liability did the House of Lords establish in Donoghue v Stevenson [1932]?

options.A: A manufacturer owes a duty of care to the ultimate consumer of their product to take reasonable care in its manufacture, regardless of any contractual relationship between them
options.B: A manufacturer is strictly liable for all damage caused by their products, without need to prove negligence or fault
options.C: A manufacturer owes a duty of care only to the purchaser of their product with whom they are in privity of contract
options.D: A manufacturer's duty of care in respect of product safety is limited to the immediate supplier in the distribution chain

questions.explanation: In Donoghue v Stevenson [1932], Lord Atkin held (by a majority of 3-2) that a manufacturer owes a duty of care to the ultimate consumer who would foreseeably use or consume their product, to take reasonable care in its manufacture to ensure it does not cause harm. Crucially, this duty arises regardless of any contractual relationship — it is a duty in tort, not contract — which was the key issue as Mrs Donoghue had not purchased the ginger beer herself.

questions.question: What did the Irish Supreme Court confirm in Power v Bedford Motor Company and Harris Brothers [1959]?

options.A: That strict liability for defective products applies to all repairers and suppliers of motor vehicles without need to prove negligence
options.B: That the Donoghue v Stevenson duty of care applies to repairers and suppliers of products, extending to a consumer who foreseeably relies on the quality of their workmanship or supply
options.C: That a car manufacturer owes a duty of care to all road users who might foreseeably be affected by a defect in a vehicle sold by its dealers
options.D: That a supplier who purchases goods for resale automatically assumes responsibility for defects originally created by the manufacturer

questions.explanation: Power v Bedford Motor Company and Harris Brothers [1959] is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority on common law product liability. The plaintiff's car, recently serviced by the defendant, had a wheel collapse due to a defective nut. The Supreme Court applied and confirmed the Donoghue v Stevenson principle, holding that the duty of care extends to repairers and suppliers of products — not just manufacturers — where consumers foreseeably rely on their skill and workmanship.

questions.question: Why was Mrs Donoghue unable to bring a claim in contract against the manufacturer of the ginger beer in Donoghue v Stevenson [1932]?

options.A: Because the applicable limitation period had expired before she could commence proceedings
options.B: Because she had signed a disclaimer when she entered the café where the ginger beer was served
options.C: Because she had not purchased the ginger beer herself and therefore was not in privity of contract with the manufacturer
options.D: Because the manufacturer had gone into liquidation and could not be sued before the House of Lords decision

questions.explanation: Mrs Donoghue was unable to sue in contract because she had not purchased the ginger beer — a friend had bought it for her. Under the doctrine of privity of contract, only a party to a contract may sue to enforce it. Since she had no contractual relationship with the manufacturer, she could not rely on any implied warranty of fitness. This is why the House of Lords had to consider whether a tortious duty of care existed between manufacturer and ultimate consumer.

questions.question: What is the 'intermediate inspection defence' in common law product liability?

options.A: A defence where a government regulatory authority inspected and approved the product as safe before it reached the consumer
options.B: A defence that removes the manufacturer's liability where the retailer should have conducted routine quality checks before offering the product for sale
options.C: A rule that a manufacturer owes no duty to a consumer who fails to read the product instructions before use
options.D: Where the manufacturer had reason to anticipate that the product would be inspected before reaching the consumer, and a reasonable inspection would have revealed the defect, the duty of care to the ultimate consumer may be discharged or reduced

questions.explanation: The intermediate inspection defence applies where a manufacturer could reasonably anticipate that the product would be inspected by an intermediate party (such as a retailer, employer, or professional) before reaching the ultimate consumer, and where a reasonable inspection would have detected the defect. In such circumstances, the manufacturer's duty to the ultimate consumer may be discharged or reduced. The defence was unavailable in Donoghue because the ginger beer was in a sealed, opaque bottle that precluded inspection.

questions.question: In which circumstance is the intermediate inspection defence unavailable to a product manufacturer in common law product liability?

options.A: Where the product is one that is foreseeably passed directly from manufacturer to consumer without inspection, such as in a sealed, opaque container
options.B: Where the product is sold exclusively through online platforms rather than through physical retail outlets
options.C: Where the consumer is a commercial entity rather than a private individual consumer
options.D: Where the defect was caused by a component manufactured by a third-party supplier rather than by the defendant directly

questions.explanation: The intermediate inspection defence is unavailable where the product is one that is foreseeably passed directly from manufacturer to consumer without any intermediate inspection. The paradigm example from Donoghue v Stevenson is a sealed, opaque bottle — the contents could not be inspected by an intermediate party before reaching the consumer. In such cases, the manufacturer cannot rely on the possibility of intermediate inspection to discharge their duty of care to the ultimate consumer.

questions.question: Which Irish case considered the common law duty to warn in product liability?

options.A: Power v Bedford Motor Company and Harris Brothers [1959], which held that repairers must warn consumers of all defects identified during a service
options.B: Kearney v Paul and Vincent [1987], where the High Court held that where a product poses a foreseeable risk to certain users, a failure to warn adequately may constitute a breach of the manufacturer's duty of care
options.C: Donoghue v Stevenson [1932], where Lord Atkin held that the duty to warn of risks is a separate and independent duty from the duty to manufacture carefully
options.D: Dineen v DePuy International [2017], where the court held that medical device manufacturers have an absolute duty to warn of all discovered risks in device documentation

questions.explanation: Kearney v Paul and Vincent [1987] is the Irish authority on the common law duty to warn in product liability. The High Court considered the adequacy of warnings accompanying a product and held that where a product poses a foreseeable risk to certain users, a failure to warn adequately of that risk may constitute a breach of the manufacturer's duty of care. The duty to warn is a distinct aspect of the manufacturer's duty under Donoghue v Stevenson.

questions.question: The Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 transposes which EU Directive into Irish law?

options.A: Directive 2024/2853/EU on the liability of producers for defective products, replacing the original 1985 regime
options.B: Directive 1999/44/EC on certain aspects of the sale of consumer goods and associated guarantees
options.C: Council Directive 85/374/EEC on the approximation of the laws of member states concerning liability for defective products
options.D: Directive 2001/95/EC on general product safety and the obligations of producers and distributors

questions.explanation: The Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 transposes Council Directive 85/374/EEC — the original EU Product Liability Directive — into Irish law. The Directive was adopted in 1985 to harmonise product liability law across EU member states. Ireland implemented it through the 1991 Act. The original 1985 Directive is now being replaced by the new EU Product Liability Directive 2024 (Directive 2024/2853), which Ireland must transpose by December 2026.

questions.question: What type of liability does the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 impose on producers of defective products?

options.A: Liability based on negligence, requiring the plaintiff to prove the producer failed to exercise reasonable care in manufacture or design
options.B: Absolute liability, with no defences available to the producer regardless of the circumstances of production or marketing
options.C: Liability based on the res ipsa loquitur principle, where the existence of a defect raises a rebuttable presumption of negligence
options.D: Strict liability — the plaintiff proves the product was defective and caused damage, without needing to prove the producer was negligent

questions.explanation: The Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 imposes strict liability on producers. The plaintiff does not need to prove negligence — they need only prove the damage, the defect, and the causal link between the defect and the damage (section 4). This is a significant departure from the common law, where negligence must be proved. However, the Act is not absolute liability — producers have access to six defences under section 6.

questions.question: Under section 2(3) of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, which of the following persons qualifies as a 'producer' for the purposes of strict liability?

options.A: A person who presents themselves as the producer of the product by affixing their own name or trade mark to it, even if they did not manufacture it
options.B: A retailer who sells the product to the public in the ordinary course of their commercial retail business
options.C: A person who rents the product to a consumer under a short-term hire or leasing agreement
options.D: A logistics company that transports the product between the EU importer and the retail outlet

questions.explanation: Under section 2(3) of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, a 'producer' includes any person who presents themselves as the producer by affixing their name, trade mark, or other distinguishing feature to the product — commonly called an 'own-brander'. This means a retailer who sells a product under their own brand name, even though they did not manufacture it, may be treated as a producer and face strict liability under the Act.

questions.question: When may a supplier of a product be treated as a 'producer' and incur strict liability under the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991?

options.A: Where the supplier receives a higher profit margin from the sale of the product than the actual manufacturer
options.B: Where the supplier cannot or does not identify the actual manufacturer when requested to do so by the plaintiff
options.C: Where the supplier has sold the product for commercial gain rather than in a non-profit or charitable context
options.D: Where the supplier has modified, adapted, or repackaged the product in any way before making it available to the consumer

questions.explanation: Under section 2(3) of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, a supplier who cannot or does not identify the actual manufacturer when required to do so may be treated as a producer and face strict liability. This provision protects plaintiffs who are unable to identify the manufacturer — they can sue the supplier, who then faces strict liability unless they can identify the manufacturer within a reasonable time. Mere sale for profit or adaptation are not by themselves sufficient.

questions.question: Under section 5 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, when is a product 'defective'?

options.A: When it does not conform to the technical specifications or applicable regulatory standards for that type of product
options.B: When it causes harm to any person who comes into contact with it, whether or not they were the intended user of the product
options.C: When it does not provide the safety which a person is entitled to expect, taking into account all the circumstances
options.D: When a design or manufacturing flaw is identified by the producer after the product has been placed on the market

questions.explanation: Under section 5 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, a product is defective when it does not provide the safety which a person is entitled to expect, taking into account all the circumstances. This is the 'consumer expectation test' — the question is not whether the product met technical standards but whether it met the level of safety that consumers are reasonably entitled to expect. This test was applied in Dineen v DePuy International [2017] and A v National Blood Authority [2001].

questions.question: In A v National Blood Authority [2001], applying the consumer expectation test to blood contaminated with hepatitis C, what did Burton J hold?

options.A: That blood transfusion products are exempt from the EU Product Liability Directive because they involve an inherently biological and uncontrollable process
options.B: That the development risk defence was available because the hepatitis C contamination risk was not fully known at the relevant time of supply
options.C: That plaintiffs claiming under the strict liability regime must also prove negligence when the defective product is a biological material
options.D: That where a product is presented as safe, consumers are entitled to expect it is safe, and a product defective in ways the consumer could not be expected to know about is defective under the Directive

questions.explanation: In A v National Blood Authority [2001], Burton J applied the consumer expectation test and held that blood supplied for transfusion was presented as safe. Consumers were entitled to expect that it was safe, and blood contaminated with hepatitis C — in ways the consumer could not be expected to know about or guard against — was defective under the Directive. Importantly, the court also held that the development risk defence was NOT available because the risk of contamination was known, even if the means to screen for it was not yet available.

questions.question: Dineen v DePuy International [2017] is the landmark Irish authority on which aspect of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991?

options.A: The application of the section 5 consumer expectation test — the court found that metal-on-metal hip implants releasing metal particles were defective because patients were entitled to expect that their implants would not contaminate their bodies
options.B: The definition of 'producer' under section 2(3), particularly regarding the liability of EU distributors who import medical devices from non-EU manufacturers
options.C: The scope of the development risk defence under section 6(e), holding it was available because the risk of metal particle release was not scientifically discoverable at the time of manufacture
options.D: The section 7 limitation period, determining when a plaintiff's knowledge of the defect, damage, and producer identity triggers the three-year clock

questions.explanation: Dineen v DePuy International [2017] is the leading Irish case on the consumer expectation test under section 5 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991. The case concerned metal-on-metal hip replacement implants that released metal particles into the bloodstream. The High Court applied the consumer expectation test and found the products were defective — patients were entitled to expect that their implants would not cause metal contamination of their bodies. The case is the landmark Irish authority on how the defect test applies to medical devices.

questions.question: Under section 4 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, what must the plaintiff prove to succeed in a product liability claim?

options.A: That the producer was negligent in the design or manufacture of the product and that this negligence caused the damage
options.B: The damage suffered; the defect in the product; and the causal link between the defect and the damage
options.C: The defect in the product; the identity of the producer; and the financial loss suffered as a direct result of the defective product
options.D: That the product failed to conform to applicable European safety standards and that this non-conformity caused the harm suffered

questions.explanation: Under section 4 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, the plaintiff bears the burden of proving three things: (1) the damage; (2) the defect in the product; and (3) the causal link between the defect and the damage. Crucially, the plaintiff does not need to prove negligence — this is the essence of the strict liability regime. Proving the identity of the producer separately is not a section 4 requirement, though it will be necessary in practice to identify the defendant.

questions.question: What must a producer establish to succeed on the development risk defence under section 6(e) of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991?

options.A: That the product was approved by relevant regulatory authorities before being placed on the market at the relevant time
options.B: That the risk that caused the damage was identified during production but could not practically be eliminated given the technology available at the time
options.C: That the state of scientific and technical knowledge at the time the product was put into circulation was not such that the defect could have been discovered
options.D: That the producer conducted all industry-standard testing and complied with applicable regulatory requirements at the time of manufacture

questions.explanation: The development risk defence under section 6(e) of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 requires the producer to prove that the state of scientific and technical knowledge at the time the product was put into circulation was not such that the defect could have been discovered. This is a high threshold — the defence is about genuinely undiscoverable risks, not simply risks that the producer chose not to investigate or that were known but technically difficult to detect.

questions.question: Following A v National Blood Authority [2001], in what circumstances is the development risk defence NOT available to a producer?

options.A: Where the product was subject to mandatory regulatory approval before being placed on the market by the producer
options.B: Where the plaintiff suffered personal injury rather than property damage as a result of the defective product
options.C: Where the producer failed to conduct scientific testing that was reasonably available and industry-standard at the time
options.D: Where the risk was known but the ability to screen for or eliminate it was not available — the defence is unavailable for known but undetectable risks

questions.explanation: Following A v National Blood Authority [2001], the development risk defence is NOT available where the risk was known — even if the technology to screen for or eliminate it was not yet available. The defence is available only for risks that were genuinely undiscoverable given the state of scientific knowledge at the relevant time. Known risks, however difficult to address technically, fall outside the defence. This is a narrow construction of the development risk defence that significantly limits its scope.

questions.question: Under section 6 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, a producer is not liable if they did not put the product into circulation. Which scenario would most clearly establish this defence?

options.A: A product stolen from the manufacturer's warehouse before being released for distribution is subsequently sold by the thief and causes harm to the purchaser
options.B: A product sold exclusively in markets outside the EU subsequently re-enters the Irish market through parallel importing and causes harm
options.C: A product sold for commercial use causes harm when a consumer misuses it for a purpose for which it was not designed or intended
options.D: A product is withdrawn from circulation by the manufacturer under a safety recall but a stockpile remains with a distributor who continues selling it

questions.explanation: The clearest example of the 'not put into circulation' defence under section 6 of the 1991 Act is where the product was stolen from the manufacturer before they voluntarily released it for distribution. In such a case the manufacturer never put the product into circulation — the act of the thief, not the manufacturer, introduced it to the market. By contrast, a product that has been put into circulation but was later recalled, or sold in other markets, would generally not attract this defence.

questions.question: Under section 7 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, within what period must a plaintiff bring their claim from the relevant trigger date?

options.A: Six years from the date on which the defective product was first placed on the market by the producer
options.B: Three years from the date on which the plaintiff became aware — or ought reasonably to have become aware — of the damage, the defect, and the identity of the producer
options.C: One year from the date on which the personal injury or property damage first became apparent to the plaintiff or their medical adviser
options.D: Two years from the date on which the plaintiff first sought medical or legal advice in relation to the damage suffered

questions.explanation: Under section 7 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, the plaintiff must bring their claim within three years from the date on which they became aware — or ought reasonably to have become aware — of the damage, the defect, and the identity of the producer. All three elements of awareness must be present before the three-year period begins. There is also an absolute longstop of ten years from the date on which the producer put the specific product that caused the damage into circulation.

questions.question: What absolute longstop period is provided under section 7 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991?

options.A: Six years from the date of first knowledge of the damage and defect, regardless of any date of knowledge arguments
options.B: Twenty years from the date on which the specific product that caused the damage was placed into circulation by the producer
options.C: Ten years from the date on which the producer put the specific product that caused the damage into circulation
options.D: Fifteen years from the date of purchase of the defective product by the plaintiff or the plaintiff's predecessor in title

questions.explanation: Section 7 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 provides an absolute longstop of ten years from the date on which the producer put the specific product that caused the damage into circulation. This longstop applies regardless of the plaintiff's date of knowledge — once ten years have elapsed from the product being placed into circulation, no claim can be brought under the Act. The EU Product Liability Directive 2024 extends this to twenty-five years for latent personal injury from implantable medical devices.

questions.question: What does section 10 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 provide regarding the exclusion of a producer's liability?

options.A: It allows producers to limit their liability to the replacement value of the defective product where this is disclosed clearly in the terms of sale
options.B: It permits exclusion of liability for property damage but prohibits exclusion of liability for personal injury or death caused by defective products
options.C: It allows producers to exclude liability through clearly worded contractual terms provided these are specifically brought to the consumer's attention at the point of purchase
options.D: It provides that the liability imposed by the Act cannot be excluded or limited by any contractual term, notice, or other provision whatsoever

questions.explanation: Section 10 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 provides that the liability imposed by the Act cannot be excluded or limited by any contractual term, notice, or other provision. This is an absolute prohibition — unlike some other statutory liability regimes, there is no scope for partial exclusion or limitation. A contractual clause purporting to exclude or limit liability under the Act is void. This reflects the mandatory nature of the EU Product Liability Directive's consumer protection framework.

questions.question: What was the first major extension of scope in the EU Product Liability Directive 2024 (Directive 2024/2853) compared to the original 1985 Directive?

options.A: The inclusion of software, artificial intelligence systems, and digital products within the definition of 'product' subject to strict liability
options.B: The extension of strict liability to cover harm caused by negligent advice or services associated with a physical product
options.C: The inclusion of second-hand and refurbished products within the scope of the Directive, making sellers of used goods subject to strict liability
options.D: The extension of the Directive's scope to cover harm suffered by workers during the manufacturing process as well as by end consumers

questions.explanation: The first major extension of scope in the EU Product Liability Directive 2024 was the inclusion of software, artificial intelligence systems, and digital products within the definition of 'product'. The original 1985 Directive applied to physical products and left questions of liability for software, algorithms, AI systems, and digital content largely unaddressed. The new Directive expressly includes these products, meaning developers of AI systems that cause harm through a defect in their algorithm may face strict liability.

questions.question: What change did the EU Product Liability Directive 2024 make regarding primary agricultural produce?

options.A: It introduced specific strict liability for primary agricultural produce for the first time, subject to a distinct contributory negligence defence for primary producers
options.B: It removed the option that allowed member states to exclude primary agricultural produce from the strict liability regime, bringing all agricultural produce within scope across the EU
options.C: It introduced a separate, less onerous liability regime specifically for primary agricultural produce distinct from that applying to manufactured goods
options.D: It extended the scope of the agricultural exclusion to include all food processing, packaging, and distribution operations beyond primary production

questions.explanation: The EU Product Liability Directive 2024 removed the option that had allowed member states under the original 1985 Directive to exclude primary agricultural produce from the strict liability regime. Under the 1985 Directive, member states could choose whether to include agricultural produce within scope — some did, some did not (Ireland had not exercised the exclusion). The 2024 Directive removes this option entirely, making all primary agricultural produce subject to the strict liability regime across the EU.

questions.question: Under the existing Irish regime (Liability for Defective Products Act 1991), what minimum threshold applies to property damage claims, and what change does the EU Product Liability Directive 2024 make?

options.A: A minimum threshold of €1,000 applies; the 2024 Directive raises it to €2,500 to focus the regime on significant property damage claims
options.B: A minimum threshold of approximately €250 applies; the 2024 Directive doubles the threshold to €500 to align all member states
options.C: A minimum threshold of approximately €445 applies; the 2024 Directive removes it entirely, making all quantifiable property damage above zero potentially recoverable
options.D: A minimum threshold of €2,000 applies; the 2024 Directive removes it only for personal injury claims, retaining it for property damage claims

questions.explanation: Under the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 (implementing the 1985 Directive), property damage claims under the Act require that the property damage exceeded a minimum threshold of approximately €445. The EU Product Liability Directive 2024 removes this minimum threshold entirely — all quantifiable property damage above zero is potentially recoverable under the new regime. This removes a barrier that had prevented minor property damage claims from being brought under the strict liability framework.

questions.question: What change did the EU Product Liability Directive 2024 make to the burden of proof for complex products such as pharmaceuticals, AI systems, and sophisticated medical devices?

options.A: It shifted the full burden of proof in relation to defect and causation from the plaintiff to the producer for all product liability claims under the new Directive
options.B: It required all producers of complex products to conduct and publicly disclose post-market surveillance testing to assist plaintiffs in establishing defects
options.C: It introduced joint and several liability for all parties in the supply chain of complex products, removing the need to identify the specific producer responsible
options.D: It allows national courts to presume the existence of a defect or a causal link between defect and damage where it is excessively difficult for the claimant to prove either, given the complexity of the product

questions.explanation: The EU Product Liability Directive 2024 introduced a significant modification to the burden of proof for complex products. Where it is excessively difficult for the claimant to prove the existence of a defect or the causal link between the defect and damage — given the technical and scientific complexity of the product (such as pharmaceuticals, sophisticated medical devices, or AI systems) — national courts may presume the existence of the defect or the causal link. This does not shift the full burden to producers but creates a facilitative presumption for claimants facing evidential difficulties.

questions.question: How did the EU Product Liability Directive 2024 narrow the development risk defence specifically in relation to software and AI systems?

options.A: It provides that a software update or new version of an AI system may constitute a new placing on the market, meaning the development risk defence runs only from the latest update, not the original product release
options.B: It completely removed the development risk defence for all software and AI products, making their developers strictly liable for all defects regardless of whether they were discoverable
options.C: It introduced a rebuttable presumption that all software defects were discoverable at the time of first release, effectively negating the development risk defence for all digital products
options.D: It limited the development risk defence to physical manufactured products only, automatically excluding it from any claim involving software embedded in or controlling a hardware device

questions.explanation: The EU Product Liability Directive 2024 narrowed the development risk defence for software and AI systems by providing that a software update or a new version of an AI system may constitute a new placing on the market. This means the development risk defence runs only from the date of the latest update — not from the date of the original product release. For evolving software products that are continuously updated, this significantly restricts the temporal scope of the development risk defence.

questions.question: What change did the EU Product Liability Directive 2024 make to the longstop limitation period for latent personal injury claims arising from implantable medical devices?

options.A: It extended the longstop from ten years to fifteen years for all product liability claims brought under the new Directive
options.B: It extended the longstop from ten years to twenty-five years for latent personal injury claims arising from implantable medical devices and similar products
options.C: It introduced a separate thirty-year longstop applicable to all claims involving defective pharmaceutical products and clinical devices
options.D: It removed the longstop entirely for latent personal injury claims where the producer was found to have deliberately concealed the defect from regulatory authorities

questions.explanation: The EU Product Liability Directive 2024 extended the longstop limitation period from ten years to twenty-five years for latent personal injury claims arising from implantable medical devices and similar products. This addresses the particular problem that conditions caused by implantable devices — such as the metal-on-metal hip implants at issue in Dineen v DePuy — may not manifest for many years after implantation, meaning the ten-year longstop was inadequate for such claims. The extension to twenty-five years significantly broadens the window for bringing latent injury claims.

questions.question: By when must Ireland transpose the EU Product Liability Directive 2024 into national law?

options.A: By June 2025, which has already passed, making the new regime immediately directly applicable in Irish courts
options.B: By December 2025, requiring the Oireachtas to enact implementing legislation during the current Dáil term
options.C: By December 2026, with the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 continuing to apply until the new implementing legislation is enacted
options.D: By December 2028, with an extended implementation period granted given the complexity of the legislative changes required

questions.explanation: Ireland must transpose the EU Product Liability Directive 2024 (Directive 2024/2853) by December 2026. Until that transposition is completed, the existing Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 — which implements the original 1985 Directive — continues to apply in Ireland. FE-1 candidates must therefore know both the current law under the 1991 Act and the key changes that will be introduced by the incoming reform.

questions.question: Under the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, which of the following persons may be treated as a 'producer' and face strict liability?

options.A: A person who provided warehouse storage facilities for the product in transit between the manufacturer and the EU importer
options.B: A retailer who sells the product to the public in the ordinary course of their retail trade without modification
options.C: A person who imports a product from another country within the European Union into Ireland for sale
options.D: A person who imports a product from outside the European Union into the EU for the purpose of sale or distribution within the EU

questions.explanation: Under section 2(3) of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, a person who imports a product from outside the European Union into the EU for the purpose of sale or distribution within the EU is treated as a 'producer' and faces strict liability. This provision ensures that non-EU manufacturers — who might otherwise be beyond the reach of Irish courts — have an EU-based party (the importer) against whom consumers can bring claims. Importing from within the EU does not trigger producer status.

questions.question: In Boyle v McDonald's Restaurants Ltd [1994], what issue did the Irish High Court consider in the context of product liability?

options.A: Whether a hot coffee spill causing burns to a consumer constituted a product defect — specifically, what level of safety a consumer was entitled to expect from the product
options.B: Whether McDonald's as a franchisor could be held vicariously liable for the product liability defaults of its franchisee's individual restaurant
options.C: Whether the preparation of food in a fast-food restaurant constituted a manufacturing process attracting strict liability under the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991
options.D: Whether the three-year limitation period under section 7 began from the date of the injury or from the date on which the consumer became aware of the alleged defect

questions.explanation: In Boyle v McDonald's Restaurants Ltd [1994], the Irish High Court considered whether a hot coffee spill causing burns to a consumer constituted a product defect under the consumer expectation test. The key question was what level of safety the consumer was entitled to expect from a cup of hot coffee. The case raises the central policy question in product liability: how to calibrate consumer expectations appropriately without making producers liable for obvious or known risks associated with their products.

questions.question: Under section 6 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, under which defence would a producer NOT be liable where the defect in their product was attributable to compliance with a mandatory regulatory requirement imposed by a public authority?

options.A: The development risk defence — because regulatory compliance demonstrates the state of knowledge was not such that the defect could be avoided
options.B: The mandatory legal requirement defence — a producer is not liable where the defect is attributable to compliance with a mandatory legal requirement imposed by a public authority
options.C: The product not in circulation defence — because a product placed on the market pursuant to regulatory compulsion was not voluntarily released into circulation
options.D: The own-supplier defence — because the regulatory authority that imposed the requirement shares responsibility for any resulting defect in the product

questions.explanation: Section 6(a) of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 provides a defence where the defect is attributable to compliance with any requirement imposed by or under any national law or any EU Community act. This 'mandatory legal requirement' defence recognises that if the producer had no choice but to comply with a statutory requirement that caused the defect, it would be unjust to impose strict liability upon them. The development risk defence addresses a different situation — one of genuine undiscoverability rather than regulatory compulsion.

questions.question: Under section 6 of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991, when is the producer of a component part NOT liable for damage caused by the defective finished product in which that component was incorporated?

options.A: Where the component producer supplied the component to a distributor rather than directly to the manufacturer of the finished product
options.B: Where the component was designed and manufactured to the highest available technical standard at the time of its production
options.C: Where the defect in the finished product is attributable to the design of the larger product or to instructions given by the manufacturer of that larger product, rather than to any defect in the component itself
options.D: Where the component producer had no knowledge that their component would be incorporated into the specific type of finished product that ultimately caused the damage

questions.explanation: Section 6(f) of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 provides that the producer of a component part is not liable where: (a) the defect in the finished product is attributable to the design of the larger product in which the component was incorporated; or (b) the defect is attributable to the instructions given by the manufacturer of the larger product. This component defence ensures that a component manufacturer is not held strictly liable for a defect that was caused by the design decisions or manufacturing instructions of the producer of the finished product.
